concepts.description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.
concepts.notes: The 'by tens' part is introduced via Counting Parade's Tens Parade challenge variant. The 'by ones to 100' extension is exercised via Counting Parade's Long Parade sub-mode (no separate lesson; same procedure as K.CC.4a at higher number ranges).

concepts.description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence.
Read and write numbers using base ten numerals from 0 to 20.
concepts.notes: Per-instance mastery — each numeral has its own ConceptState. Concept-wide mastery rolls up when all 21 instances are mastered. The lesson covers one numeral ('5') as worked example; the trace procedure generalizes to the other 20.

concepts.description: When counting objects, say the numbers in the standard order. Pair each quantity of objects with one and only one number, and each number with the correct quantity of objects.
concepts.notes: One-to-one correspondence — the bedrock counting procedure. First lesson a K kid sees.

concepts.description: Understand that the last number said represents the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of arrangement or order in which they were counted.
concepts.notes: Cardinality — the insight that the last count IS how many.

concepts.description: Understand that each successive number refers to a quantity that is one greater than the previous number.
concepts.notes: Successor principle. Folded into K.CC.4a/4b's lessons — successor is implicit in counting and add-to stories. Exercises-only mastery: kid demonstrates the +1 insight by completing counting passes AND story add-to rounds.

concepts.description: Use counting to answer questions about 'how many.' Count up to 20 in line/array/circle, 10 in scattered configuration. Count out a given number.
concepts.notes: Arrangement variations + count-out. Exercises-only because the procedure is K.CC.4a/4b applied across visual contexts. Mastery via kid demonstrating multiple arrangements.

concepts.description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number in another, by matching or counting (up to 10 objects).
Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.
Represent addition and subtraction with objects, fingers, mental images, simple drawings, or sounds (e.g., act out, use clapping, use verbal explanations, expressions, or equations).

siblingCreditRules.rationale: K.OA.2 word-problem solving inherently exercises scene-manipulation representation. Crediting it as one demonstration mode makes K.OA.1 mastery reachable for kids who prefer tile-select but engage word problems successfully.

concepts.notes: Multi-representational concept. Exercises-only because no single activity 'teaches' representation — the kid demonstrates it by using multiple modes naturally during play. Sounds/clapping mode dropped per design decisions (no clapping in the product). Hybrid mastery rule (added 2026-05-30): K.OA.2 Practicing state counts as the scene-manipulation demonstration mode, so kids who prefer tile-select can still reach K.OA.1 mastery by demonstrating 1 additional mode (notebook OR equation).

concepts.description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems within 10.
Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way.
concepts.notes: Lesson also introduces the ten-frame representation (bundled — no separate concept for the tool).

concepts.description: Make sums of 10 using any number from 1 to 9.
Fluently add and subtract using numbers within 5.
concepts.notes: Per-instance mastery — each addition/subtraction fact within 5 has its own ConceptState. 21 addition + 21 subtraction = 42 instance keys covering all sums and differences from 0 to 5.

concepts.description: Compose and decompose numbers from 11–19 into ten ones and some further ones (e.g., 18 = 10 + 8).
concepts.notes: Conceptual bridge from 'counting things' to 'place value.' Foundation for 1st-grade Place Value Bundling and 2nd-grade Place Value Vault.

concepts.description: Describe measurable attributes of objects (length, weight). Describe several measurable attributes of a single object.
Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common, to see which has more of/less of the attribute.
Classify objects into given categories; count the numbers of objects in each category and sort the categories by count (limit category counts to ≤ 10).
Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes. Describe relative positions using terms such as above, below, beside, in front of, behind, next to.
concepts.notes: Two lessons cover the standard. Primary (lesson-k-g-1) introduces above/behind/next-to on first session. Secondary (lesson-k-g-1b) introduces below/in-front-of/beside on second session. Together they explicitly cover all six position words — no first-encounter cold for any word.

concepts.description: Correctly name shapes regardless of their orientations or overall sizes.
Identify shapes as two-dimensional ('flat') or three-dimensional ('solid').
Analyze, compare, and sort 2D and 3D shapes, using informal language to describe similarities, differences, and other attributes.
Model and create shapes from components such as sticks and clay balls.
Compose simple shapes to form larger shapes.
concepts.notes: Fires via intra_round_lesson_chain in Phase 2 of the same first round as K.G.5 (per build-a-habitat's intra-round chain declaration).
